title: Multimodal Fusion Architectures
summary: Combining different data types (text + image, time series + text, sensor + reference) into a unified model. Three main strategies: early fusion (concatenate inputs), late fusion (separate encoders, merge outputs), and cross-attention fusion (separate encoders, cross-attend between them). Cross-attention fusion is the most powerful and connects to your core application interest.
key_insights: Early fusion: concatenate raw inputs before the model. Simple but forces a single tokenization for different data types.
Late fusion: separate encoders produce separate representations, combined at the end (concat, sum, MLP). Each modality gets its own encoder but limited interaction.
Cross-attention fusion: separate encoders, cross-attention between them. Most flexible — each modality can query the other. This is the dual-encoder pattern from your assessment discussion.
Bidirectional cross-attention: modality A attends to B AND B attends to A. Most powerful but most expensive.
Asymmetric cross-attention: one modality queries the other (not vice versa). Appropriate when one modality is the 'question' and the other is the 'knowledge'.

mathematical_core.late_fusion: h = combine(encoder_A(a), encoder_B(b)) where combine = concat or add or MLP
mathematical_core.cross_attention_fusion: h_A' = CrossAttn(Q=h_A, K=h_B, V=h_B) — A attends to B
mathematical_core.bidirectional: h_A' = CrossAttn(Q=h_A, K=h_B, V=h_B), h_B' = CrossAttn(Q=h_B, K=h_A, V=h_A)

related: TRANSFORMER.CROSS_ATTENTION — cross-attention fusion uses this mechanism

open_questions: OQ1 (from TRANSFORMER.RESIDUALS): Should residual bypass cross-attention in dual-encoder? Directly relevant here — determines how much each modality preserves its own representation vs absorbs the other.
notes: This is the theoretical home for the dual-encoder cross-attention application idea discussed extensively during assessment. The sensor + knowledge base, query + documents, and data analysis patterns all live here.
